Australia's Wesfarmers half-year profit beats estimates
data.description:
Feb 19 (Reuters) - Wesfarmers, Australia's biggest non-food retailer, reported a 9.3% rise in its first-half profit on Thursday, beating estimates due to strong performances at its Bunnings, Kmart Group, and WesCEF divisions.

The apparel-to-lithium conglomerate's net profit after tax for the six months ended December 31 came in at A$1.60 billion ($1.13 billion), higher than the Visible Alpha consensus estimate of A$1.56 billion and above the A$1.47 billion reported last year.

Revenue at Wesfarmers' top-performing division, hardware retailer Bunnings, rose 4.2% to A$10.71 billion in the six months to December 31, driven by sales growth across all product categories and operating regions, and in both consumer and commercial segments.

Its budget department store chain, Kmart, also benefited from the strength of its Anko products, which drove its half-year sales 3.3% higher to A$6.31 billion.

The Perth-based conglomerate, which also co-owns a lithium project in Western Australia with Chile's SQM, declared an interim dividend of 102 Australian cents per share, higher than 95 Australian cents apiece last year.

($1 = 1.4203 Australian dollars)

(Reporting by Sneha Kumar and Rajasik Mukherjee in Bengaluru; Editing by Maju Samuel and Alan Barona)

Booking 4Q Sales, Profit Rise as More Travelers Make Reservations
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton


Booking recorded higher sales and profit in the fourth quarter as room nights and bookings increased.

The online travel agency on Wednesday posted a profit of $1.43 billion, or $44.22 a share, compared with $1.07 billion, or $31.95 a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted per-share earnings were $48.80, ahead of the $48.69 anticipated by analysts, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose 16% to $6.35 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet forecast revenue of $6.14 billion.

Room nights increased 9% and gross bookings were up 16%.

Booking expects revenue to increase 14% to 16% in the current first quarter, and increase by a low double-digit percentage during the full year. Analysts were projecting 12% growth in the first quarter and 9.5% growth for the full year.

The company is anticipating full-year adjusted earnings per share to increase by a mid-teens percentage, while Wall Street was estimating an 18% increase.


Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-18-26 1634ET

Transurban Returns to Profit, Distribution Guidance Unchanged
data.description:
By David Winning


SYDNEY--Transurban said higher traffic on its network of global toll roads helped it to return to a statutory profit in its fiscal first half, but rising interest rates in Australia are emerging as a new headwind.

Transurban reported a net profit attributable to securityholders of 298 million Australian dollars (US$211.2 million) in the six months through December, compared to a A$47 million loss at the same stage of fiscal 2025.

Proportional toll revenue--the company's preferred measure of the performance of its roads--increased by 6.4% to A$1.99 billion in the six-month period. Proportional earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization--or Ebitda--rose by 22% to A$1.59 billion.

Transurban kept its distribution guidance for the full year steady at 69 Australian cents a security. Its first-half payout, which was pre-released, totaled A$0.34 a share.

Population growth, lower interest rates and investments all supported Transurban's improved performance in the period. Average daily traffic rose by 2.5% over the six months through December, despite bad weather in Sydney. In Australia, the fastest growth in traffic was in Melbourne, up 3.0%, reversing prior periods when it had been a relative laggard.

Transurban said its net finance costs fell to A$330 million in the six-month period, from A$444 million, reflecting lower borrowing costs compared to a year earlier.

However, Transurban is facing a changed backdrop as interest rates climb once more. The Reserve Bank of Australia this month lifted its benchmark rate by 25 basis points to 3.85%, representing the first increase in 18 months as it battles to squash a reacceleration in inflation. Economists have noted that the central bank typically raises rates more than once before hitting a pause.

Many investors consider Transurban to be one of the more defensive stocks on the Australian Securities Exchange as its tolls are linked to inflation and it has hedging in place. Transurban said 88.6% of its debt was hedged at the end of the first half.

Still, there are risks that households seeking to save money might use their car less or accept longer journey times than use toll roads, while higher interest rates could damp economic activity more broadly.

One positive development for Transurban's second-half performance is the opening of the West Gate Tunnel Project in Melbourne last in December. Transurban expects the tunnel, which connects the West Gate Freeway to the Port of Melbourne, to be well used by freight haulers.

"The 1H result reflects the accomplishment of key projects in North America and our home market of Melbourne, including the 495 Northern Extension and the opening of the West Gate Tunnel project," said Chief Executive Michelle Jablko.


Write to David Winning at david.winning@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-18-26 1607ET
